source_mechanism: Processing of personal data is lawful only if the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes (consent as the lawful basis for processing).

votes.target_mechanism: An organisation may collect, use, or disclose personal data only if the individual has given consent to the collection, use, or disclosure of the personal data for the purposes that would be considered appropriate to a reasonable person.
votes.mapping_justification: Section 13 of the PDPA (Singapore) establishes consent as a lawful basis for the processing of personal data, aligning with GDPR Article 6(1)(a) in recognizing consent as a valid legal ground. While the PDPA allows for other grounds beyond consent, Section 13 specifically mirrors the principle that consent must be obtained for data processing activities, making it the closest functional equivalent.

votes.target_mechanism: Section 13 of the PDPA establishes consent as a primary condition for the collection, use, and disclosure of personal data, requiring organizations to obtain an individual's consent before processing their personal data except in specified circumstances.
votes.mapping_justification: Both Article 6(1)(a) GDPR and Section 13 PDPA establish consent as a lawful basis for processing personal data. The GDPR provision makes consent a foundational lawful basis for any processing, while Section 13 PDPA similarly requires consent as a primary condition for collection and use of personal data, making them functionally analogous mechanisms for consent-based data processing.

votes.target_mechanism: An organization must obtain the consent of the individual for the collection, use, or disclosure of their personal data for purposes that a reasonable person would consider appropriate in the circumstances. This provision establishes consent as a primary legal basis for processing activities under the PDPA.
votes.mapping_justification: Both GDPR Article 6(1)(a) and PDPA Section 13 identify consent as a fundamental requirement for the lawful processing of personal data. They share the core principle that processing is permitted when the individual has voluntarily agreed to the specific purposes of the data handling.

votes.target_mechanism: PDPA s13 requires that an organisation obtain the individual’s consent for the collection, use or disclosure of the individual’s personal data, and that such consent be given for the specific purposes for which the personal data is collected, used or disclosed.
votes.mapping_justification: This maps to GDPR Article 6(1)(a) because both frameworks treat the data subject’s consent as a lawful basis for processing personal data for specific purposes; PDPA s13 explicitly places consent as the required basis for collection, use and disclosure while the PDPA also contains its own set of exceptions and operational rules that differ in structure from the GDPR.

source_mechanism: Designation of the data protection officer (DPO): the controller and processor shall designate a DPO where (a) processing is carried out by a public authority, (b) the core activities require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects on a large scale, or (c) the core activities consist of large-scale processing of special categories of data.

votes.target_mechanism: The organisation must appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) if it is required to do so under the Advisory Guidelines on the Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (as amended), particularly where the organisation carries out large-scale collection, use, or disclosure of personal data, or acts as a data intermediary. The DPO must be responsible for ensuring compliance with the PDPA and be contactable by individuals.
votes.mapping_justification: Section 26A of the PDPA (Singapore) introduces the requirement for certain organisations to appoint a Data Protection Officer, mirroring the GDPR's Article 37 mandate. While the PDPA does not prescribe the exact same triggers as the GDPR (e.g., public authority, large-scale monitoring, or special categories), the underlying principle of appointing a DPO based on the scale and sensitivity of data processing aligns closely, especially in the context of large-scale or high-risk processing activities.

votes.target_mechanism: The PDPA does not contain a mandatory data protection officer designation requirement analogous to GDPR Article 37.
votes.mapping_justification: The Singapore PDPA does not impose a statutory obligation for organizations to designate a data protection officer. While the PDPA establishes data protection obligations for organizations, it does not require the appointment of a DPO as a compliance mechanism, unlike the GDPR which mandates DPO designation under specific conditions.

votes.target_mechanism: Section 11 of the PDPA requires organizations to designate at least one individual to be responsible for ensuring the organization complies with the Act and to make the business contact information of such individual available to the public.
votes.mapping_justification: Both provisions mandate the appointment of a specific individual responsible for data protection compliance within an organization. While GDPR Article 37 is conditional based on processing activities, PDPA Section 11 is a general requirement for all organizations subject to the Act.

votes.target_mechanism: The PDPA does not impose a statutory requirement to designate a Data Protection Officer (DPO). Instead, the Act’s Accountability Obligation requires organisations to implement policies and practices to ensure compliance, and the PDPC’s guidance materials recommend appointing a DPO or equivalent officer as a best-practice governance measure.
votes.mapping_justification: No direct analogue to GDPR Article 37 exists in the PDPA: the Singapore law does not mandate DPO designation based on factors like public authority status, large-scale monitoring, or processing of special categories of data. Appointment of a DPO in Singapore is a regulatory guidance/best practice rather than a statutory trigger, so there is no single PDPA clause that matches Article 37’s mandatory designation rules.

source_mechanism: Notification of a personal data breach to the supervisory authority: in the case of a personal data breach the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the breach to the competent supervisory authority, unless the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons.

votes.target_mechanism: A data controller must notify the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) of a data breach as soon as practicable, and in any case within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach, where the breach is likely to result in significant harm to affected individuals or is of a significant scale.
votes.mapping_justification: Section 26B of the PDPA (SG) establishes a data breach notification obligation to the supervisory authority (PDPC) with a 72-hour timeframe, closely mirroring GDPR's Article 33. The requirement is triggered when the breach is likely to cause significant harm or involves a significant number of individuals, which aligns with GDPR's risk-based threshold for notification.

votes.target_mechanism: Section 48C of the PDPA requires organizations to notify the Personal Data Protection Commission and affected individuals without undue delay upon discovery of a data breach that is likely to result in serious harm, establishing a mandatory breach notification regime similar to GDPR Article 33.
votes.mapping_justification: Both provisions establish mandatory notification obligations for data controllers/organizations upon discovery of a personal data breach, with notification required to the relevant supervisory authority (PDPC in Singapore, equivalent to GDPR's supervisory authorities) within a specified timeframe when the breach poses a risk to individuals' rights and freedoms.

votes.target_mechanism: Organizations are required to notify the Personal Data Protection Commission of a data breach if the breach results in, or is likely to result in, significant harm to affected individuals or affects a significant scale of individuals. The notification must be made as soon as practicable and no later than three calendar days after the organization determines that a notifiable data breach has occurred.
votes.mapping_justification: Both provisions establish a mandatory reporting timeline for data breaches to a supervisory authority. While the thresholds for notification differ slightly, both frameworks prioritize timely disclosure to regulators to mitigate risks to data subjects.

votes.target_mechanism: Under the PDPA Notifiable Data Breaches regime, organisations must notify the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) as soon as practicable when a data breach has occurred that is likely to cause significant harm to individuals or is of a significant scale; the regime also requires notification to affected individuals in such cases and prescribes the content of notices.
votes.mapping_justification: This provision is analogous to GDPR Article 33 because both require prompt notification to the supervisory authority of a personal data breach unless the breach is unlikely to result in risk to individuals; the PDPA threshold is expressed as ‘likely to cause significant harm or of significant scale’ and uses the phrase ‘as soon as practicable’ rather than a fixed 72‑hour deadline, but the mechanisms serve the same regulatory function.

source_mechanism: Security of processing: the controller and processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including as appropriate pseudonymisation and encryption, ability to ensure ongoing confidentiality/integrity/availability, ability to restore availability after an incident, and a process for regularly testing those measures.

votes.target_mechanism: Organisations must implement reasonable security arrangements to protect personal data from unauthorised access, collection, use, disclosure, copying, modification, disposal or similar risks, especially when the data is transmitted, transferred or otherwise made available outside the organisation.
votes.mapping_justification: Section 24 of the PDPA (Singapore) requires organisations to protect personal data using reasonable security measures, which aligns with GDPR Article 32's requirement for appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure data security. While less prescriptive than the GDPR, it covers the core obligation to safeguard data confidentiality, integrity, and availability, particularly in high-risk contexts such as data transfers.

votes.target_mechanism: Section 32 of the PDPA requires organizations to protect personal data by making reasonable security arrangements against unauthorized access, collection, use, disclosure, copying, modification, or disposal. Organizations must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures proportionate to the sensitivity of the personal data and the potential harm from unauthorized processing.
votes.mapping_justification: Both Article 32 GDPR and Section 32 PDPA establish mandatory security obligations for data controllers/organizations to implement technical and organizational measures protecting personal data. Both provisions require measures appropriate to the risk level and emphasize confidentiality, integrity, and availability protections, making them directly analogous security safeguard requirements.

votes.target_mechanism: The Protection Obligation requires organizations to make reasonable security arrangements to prevent unauthorised access, collection, use, disclosure, copying, modification, disposal, or similar risks to personal data in their possession or under their control.
votes.mapping_justification: Both GDPR Article 32 and PDPA Section 24 mandate that organizations implement appropriate security measures to protect personal data against unauthorized access or loss. While the GDPR provides more granular technical examples, the PDPA's Protection Obligation serves as the primary legal requirement for ensuring data security.

votes.target_mechanism: PDPA s24 requires organisations to make reasonable security arrangements to protect personal data in their possession or under their control against unauthorised access, collection, use, disclosure, copying, modification, disposal or similar risks; this is implemented through technical and organisational measures (for example access controls, encryption, staff policies and secure disposal) and risk-based safeguards.
votes.mapping_justification: GDPR Art.32’s obligation to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures (including pseudonymisation/encryption, ensuring confidentiality/integrity/availability, recovery and testing) aligns with PDPA s24’s core duty to put in place reasonable, risk‑based security arrangements to protect personal data. Both impose a risk‑based security obligation on the data controller/organisation to adopt technical and organisational controls to prevent unauthorised access and to maintain availability and integrity of data.

source_mechanism: Right of access by the data subject: the data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller confirmation as to whether or not personal data concerning him or her are being processed, and, where that is the case, access to the personal data and information about the purposes of processing, categories of data, recipients, retention period, and the existence of rights to rectification/erasure/restriction/objection.

votes.target_mechanism: Section 21 of the PDPA (Singapore) provides individuals with the right to request access to their personal data held by an organisation and to be informed of the purposes for which the data has been or may be used or disclosed.
votes.mapping_justification: This mirrors GDPR Article 15 by granting data subjects the right to confirmation of processing and access to their personal data, along with key information about processing purposes and usage. While less extensive than GDPR in scope (e.g., not explicitly listing retention periods or all rights), Section 21 is the closest functional equivalent in the PDPA framework.

votes.target_mechanism: Section 20 of the PDPA grants data subjects the right to access personal data held by organizations and to request information about how their data is being used, including purposes, categories of recipients, and retention periods.
votes.mapping_justification: Article 15 GDPR and Section 20 PDPA both establish a fundamental right of access allowing data subjects to obtain confirmation of processing and access to their personal data along with processing details. Both provisions serve the same transparency and accountability function in their respective jurisdictions.

votes.target_mechanism: Section 21 of the PDPA provides individuals with the right to request access to their personal data in the possession or under the control of an organization, as well as information about the ways in which that personal data has been used or disclosed within the past year.
votes.mapping_justification: Both provisions establish the fundamental right of a data subject to access their personal data held by an entity. While the scope of information required differs slightly, both serve the primary purpose of ensuring transparency regarding the processing of an individual's personal information.

votes.target_mechanism: Section 21 of the PDPA gives an individual the right to request access to personal data held by an organisation, including confirmation of whether the organisation is processing the data, provision of a copy of the data, and information about the purposes of collection and disclosure to third parties; the organisation must respond within the statutory timeframe and may charge a prescribed fee.
votes.mapping_justification: This maps to GDPR Article 15 because both grant a data subject/individual the right to obtain confirmation that personal data are being processed and to access the personal data and related information (purposes and classes of recipients). Notwithstanding some differences in detail (PDPA handles retention, correction and other rights under separate provisions and prescribes response procedures/fees), Section 21 is the closest PDPA analogue to the GDPR access right.
